perturbation.original_text: The Sub-Advisor shall act honestly, with due skill, care and diligence and fairly and in the best interest of the Partnership in carrying out its obligations under this Agreement and shall use all reasonable endeavours to perform its obligations under this Agreement in accordance with FSMA, the FCA Rules and any other laws, regulations, guidelines and guidance as may be in force from time to time and applicable to the Funds and their business or to the Sub-Advisor ("Applicable Law").
perturbation.changed_text: The Sub-Advisor shall act honestly and fairly in carrying out its obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirement for the sub-advisor to act with 'due skill, care, and diligence' and removes the clause that they shall 'use all reasonable endeavors to perform its obligations under this Agreement in accordance with FSMA, the FCA Rules and any other laws'. This weakens the standard of care and compliance expected of the Sub-Advisor and could lead to a violation of the FCA's Conduct of Business Sourcebook (COBS).
perturbation.law_explanation: COBS 2.1.1 R states that 'A firm must act honestly, fairly and professionally in accordance with the best interests of its client.' By removing 'due skill, care and diligence' the contract now contradicts this requirement, suggesting a lower standard of conduct is acceptable, potentially failing to act in the client's best interests or professionally.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Simmons & Simmons We're sorry but website doesn't work properly without JavaScript enabled. Please enable it to continue.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirements for the sub-advisor to act with 'due skill, care, and diligence' and to perform its obligations in accordance with FSMA, the FCA Rules, and other applicable laws. The Law Explanation states that COBS 2.1.1 R requires firms to act 'honestly, fairly and professionally in accordance with the best interests of its client.' By removing 'due skill, care and diligence' the contract suggests a lower standard of conduct is acceptable, potentially failing to act in the client's best interests or professionally. While the scraped law snippets are not helpful, the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition states that a contradiction exists when a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant, and this change falls under that definition.

perturbation.original_text: Oaktree US will be the Sub-Advisor's client for the purposes of the FCA Rules. Accordingly, in conformity with the FCA Rules, a number of additional statements and provisions are required to be included in this Agreement. Such additional statements and provisions are set out in Schedule 1 hereof ("Additional FCA Provisions"), which is hereby incorporated into and will form part of this Agreement and will apply to the services to be provided pursuant to this Agreement with effect from the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: Oaktree US will be the Sub-Advisor's client. Additional statements and provisions may be required to be included in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the statement that the agreement must conform with FCA rules, and explicitly removing the incorporation of Schedule 1 (Additional FCA Provisions), this modification introduces a legal contradiction. Schedule 1 would likely contain provisions required under the FCA rules to protect the client.
perturbation.contradicted_law: FCA Rules, specifically those relating to client agreements
perturbation.law_explanation: COBS 9.2.1R mandates certain information and protections to be included in client agreements. By suggesting that these provisions 'may' be required rather than 'are' required, and removing the schedule, the contract potentially violates these mandatory disclosure and protection requirements under the FCA.

Statutory Instruments 2001 No. 1282 (C. 48) FINANCIAL SERVICES AND MARKETS The Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Commencement No. 2) Order 2001 Made 30th March 2001 The Treasury, in exercise of the powers conferred on them by section 431(2) of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000( 1 ) (“the Act”), hereby make the following Order: 1. This Order may be cited as the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Commencement No. 2) Order 2001. 2. The day appointed for the coming into force of— (a) subsections (1) (c) and (3)(a) of section 416 of the Act; and (b) section 432(3) of and Schedule 22 to the Act, for the purpose of repealing the Insurance Brokers (Registration) Act 1977( 2 ); is 30th April 2001. Clive Betts David Jamieson Two of the Lords Commissioners of Her Majesty’s Treasury 30th March 2001 Explanatory Note (This note is not part of the Order) This Order brings into force on 30th April 2001 section 416(1)(c) and, in part, section 432(3) of, and Schedule 22 to, the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (“the Act”). These provisions repeal the Insurance Brokers (Registration) Act 1977. The Order also brings into force on 30th April 2001 section 416(3)(a) of the Act which provides that the Insurance Brokers Registration Council, a body established pursuant to the Insurance Brokers (Registration) Act 1977, ceases to exist. Note as to Earlier Commencement Orders (This note is not part of the Order) Provision Date of Commencement S.I. perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly incorporates Schedule 1, which contains "Additional FCA Provisions" required to conform with FCA rules, to protect the client. The changed text removes this explicit incorporation and suggests that these provisions 'may' be required. According to the Perturbation Explanation, Schedule 1 would likely contain provisions required under the FCA rules to protect the client, and the Law Explanation states that COBS 9.2.1R mandates certain information and protections to be included in client agreements. Thus, the omission of the explicit incorporation of FCA provisions creates a contradiction by potentially violating mandatory disclosure and protection requirements, making the contract non-compliant. The scraped law snippet 1 does not directly address the FCA requirements for client agreements, but the Perturbation and Law explanations indicate a legal obligation under the FCA which is undermined in the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: The Sub-Advisor shall maintain proper and complete records relating to the services to be provided under this Agreement for such period of time as may be required under Applicable Law, including (as applicable, in respect of the relevant Discretionary Funds) records with respect to the acquisition, holding and disposal of securities on behalf of the Funds, details of all brokers used and the aggregate dollar amount of brokerage commission paid in that regard to each broker.
perturbation.changed_text: The Sub-Advisor shall maintain records relating to the services to be provided under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the requirements that the records must be 'proper and complete', and removes specific details about record-keeping, such as those related to 'acquisition, holding and disposal of securities', as well as the details of 'all brokers used and the aggregate dollar amount of brokerage commission'. The removal of these clauses creates a legal risk, as it implies that the Sub-Advisor need not maintain records that fully comply with the requirements of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940.
perturbation.law_citation: Rule 204-2(a) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 204-2(a) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 outlines the specific books and records that investment advisers must maintain. By removing the requirement that the records be 'proper and complete' and removing specific requirements, the modified text potentially violates this rule, creating a situation where the Sub-Advisor could fail to maintain legally mandated records.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirements for 'proper and complete' records and specific details regarding record-keeping, potentially violating Rule 204-2(a) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that Part 275 of Title 17 pertains to rules and regulations under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940, suggesting the modified text does not meet legal standards as it does not provide necessary details, potentially leading to non-compliance. The Omission of legally mandated consumer protection requirements creates a legal contradiction.
